title: ZKP-Based Agent Auth to Prevent Credential Exposure
description: OpenAI's coding agent leaked GitHub passwords because agents held long-lived secrets. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit lets agents prove authorization via ZKP without ever holding or transmitting the underlying credential, eliminating the credential-exposure attack surface entirely.
source: OpenAI coding agent flaw exposed GitHub passwords

rationale.demand: The OpenAI credential leak is a single high-profile incident confirming the attack surface exists, but web search evidence failed entirely — no verified URLs, no named buyers seeking ZKP-based agent auth, and no funded competitors building this specific solution.
rationale.timing: The incident creates a news-cycle window but no standard body, regulatory deadline, or competitor launch date is cited with specifics; agent auth standards (draft-klrc-aiagent) are 18+ months from finalization, making the window real but not closing.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and HumanUniqueness map directly to the credential-less agent auth story with only minor extensions needed, and the handshake nonce binding already prevents replay — strong primitive coverage.
rationale.feasibility: 8.5 days and 78% reuse is plausible for a solo founder, but the GitHub App gateway requires ongoing maintenance, GitHub API compatibility work, and the demo value depends on GitHub actually accepting proof-gated tokens — integration risk is moderate.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive. Agent proves it holds a valid operator-signed credential with sufficient permission bits (e.g. WRITE_DATA for git push, READ_DATA for clone) without revealing the credential itself. The selective disclosure capability means the agent proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without exposing the full bitmask or the underlying secret key. Expiry enforcement ensures credentials auto-expire, eliminating the long-lived secret problem entirely.

The human developer who authorized the agent proves enrollment in the organization's Semaphore tree. This binds the agent's delegated authority to a real human identity without revealing which human, preventing unauthorized agents from self-enrolling. The nullifier provides sybil resistance — one human can't create unlimited agent authorizations within the same scope.

Models the developer→agent authorization chain. Developer (human identity) delegates a scope-narrowed credential to the coding agent. Scope narrowing ensures the agent can only get a bitwise subset of the developer's permissions (e.g. WRITE_DATA to specific repos, not SIGN_ON_BEHALF). Expiry narrowing means the agent's credential expires before or at the developer's session boundary. Max 3 hops covers developer→orchestrator→coding-agent→sub-agent chains.

On-chain verification of the handshake between human and agent. The atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) lets a gateway verify that both the human authorization and agent credential are valid before granting access to the protected resource (GitHub API). Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents a revoked agent from reusing a previously valid delegation proof.

proveHandshake() generates the proof pair that a GitHub gateway would verify before proxying API calls. createAgentCredential() with scoped permissions replaces the long-lived GitHub PAT. The existing error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, StaleProofError) already model the failure modes relevant to this use case.

new_components_needed.description: GitHub OAuth/App gateway adapter: a proxy that sits between the coding agent and GitHub API, accepts Bolyra proofs instead of PATs, verifies them, then issues short-lived (seconds) GitHub installation tokens scoped to the proven permission set. Similar pattern to the existing OpenClaw adapter but targeting GitHub's App installation token API.

Agent SDK convenience layer for coding-agent runtimes (OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, Cursor): a lightweight client that wraps proveHandshake() calls so the agent can generate proofs transparently when making GitHub API requests, without the agent framework needing to understand ZKP internals.

Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining the 'credential proxy' flow: how a verifier gateway translates a valid Bolyra proof into a short-lived platform-specific token. This is the generic pattern that GitHub is the first instance of, but applies to any API provider.

Optional: scope-registry contract that maps GitHub repository IDs to Bolyra scope identifiers, enabling nullifier-based rate limiting per repo (prevents a compromised agent from mass-cloning). Not required for MVP but strengthens the story.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. GitHub is the incumbent platform; Bolyra does not compete with it but slots underneath as the auth layer that GitHub (or any API provider) can adopt without changing their core product. The value proposition to GitHub: 'your agent ecosystem gets credential-exposure immunity without you rebuilding your auth stack — just verify Bolyra proofs at the gateway and issue scoped installation tokens.' This matches the strategy of making incumbents' existing agent ecosystems more secure rather than asking developers to switch platforms. The OpenAI coding agent incident provides urgent, concrete demand that makes the pitch timely rather than theoretical.

deliverables.name: github-gateway
deliverables.description: Express proxy that accepts Bolyra AgentPolicy proofs, verifies via on-chain registry, then mints short-lived GitHub installation tokens (scoped to proven permission bits). Maps READ_DATA→contents:read, WRITE_DATA→contents:write. Stateless — no credential storage.

deliverables.name: sdk-github-helpers
deliverables.description: createGitHubAgentCredential(operatorKey, repos, permissions, expirySeconds) convenience wrapper in @bolyra/sdk. Maps GitHub permission strings to Bolyra bitmask. Adds GitHubScopeMapper utility.

deliverables.name: e2e-demo
deliverables.description: CLI script: human enrolls → delegates to coding agent → agent clones private repo via gateway → pushes commit → proof expires → retry fails. Single README-executable flow.

deliverables.name: threat-model-doc
deliverables.description: Technical comparison: PAT-based agent auth vs Bolyra ZKP auth. Attack surface analysis referencing the OpenAI incident. Maps to OWASP Agentic Top 10 (extends existing owasp-agentic-mapping.md).

deliverables.name: github-app-manifest
deliverables.description: GitHub App configuration (app.yml) with minimal permissions. Installation guide for self-hosted gateway. Terraform optional.

risks: GitHub App rate limits on installation token minting may bottleneck high-throughput agent workflows (1 token/min/installation)
Feasibility score is lowest (18) — the gateway requires GitHub App approval for production use beyond personal repos
Web search evidence returned nothing — demand signal is inferred from one incident, not validated by market pull
Solo founder capacity: 8.5 days assumes no context-switching; realistic calendar time is 2-3 weeks
On-chain verification latency (~2-4s on Base Sepolia) adds friction vs instant PAT auth — may need off-chain verifier fallback for demo credibility
mvp.success_criteria: Demo video showing: (1) agent with expired/revoked credential gets rejected by gateway, (2) agent with valid Bolyra proof clones and pushes to a private repo, (3) no secret appears in agent memory or logs at any point. Secondary: one external developer reproduces the demo from README alone within 30 minutes.

title: Privacy-Preserving Alternative to Agent Passport / AID
description: Agent Passport and AgentIDs.org offer OAuth-like agent identity but expose agent metadata (operator, model, permissions) to every verifier. Bolyra's selective-disclosure ZKP proofs let agents prove 'I have FINANCIAL_SMALL permission' without revealing their full credential or operator identity — a clear privacy moat.
source: Agent Passport (HN) and AgentIDs.org open protocol

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs were provided — demand rests entirely on the existence of Agent Passport/AgentIDs.org without citing named buyers, funding rounds, or engagement metrics.
rationale.timing: No concrete competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the timing argument is directional but unanchored beyond general awareness that agent identity is emerging.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation) plus the SDK and IETF draft map directly with only minor modifications needed — selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask is already built.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec is well-scoped at ~9 days reusing existing primitives, but the opportunity's value depends on unvalidated demand, making the build speculative despite low technical risk.

mapped_primitives.usage: Already supports selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask — agent proves specific permission bits are set without revealing the full 8-bit bitmask or operator identity. This is the core privacy moat vs Agent Passport's plaintext metadata exposure.

Proves the human principal behind a delegated agent credential is unique within a scope (e.g., per-enterprise sybil resistance) without revealing who they are. Agent Passport has no human-binding primitive at all.

Enables human→agent→sub-agent chains with scope narrowing. An enterprise verifier confirms 'this agent was delegated FINANCIAL_SMALL by a valid human' without learning the human's identity or the agent's full permission set. Directly counters Agent Passport's flat, fully-visible delegation model.

On-chain root history buffers let verifiers check Merkle membership without querying the operator's infrastructure — removes the phone-home trust assumption that Agent Passport inherits from OAuth. Atomic handshake verification handles the full human+agent proof in one tx.

createAgentCredential + proveHandshake already produce the selective-disclosure proofs needed. The error taxonomy (InvalidPermissionError, ScopeEscalationError) maps cleanly to enterprise auth failure modes. SDK is the developer surface for the Agent Passport alternative pitch.

The IETF draft gives standards credibility against Agent Passport's ad-hoc spec. Positioning as 'the privacy-preserving alternative with a standards track' is stronger than 'just another agent ID system'.

new_components_needed.description: Agent Passport compatibility profile: a mapping document showing how Bolyra proofs can satisfy Agent Passport/AID verification endpoints. Defines a translation layer so enterprises already evaluating Agent Passport can adopt Bolyra as a drop-in privacy upgrade without changing their verifier API surface.

Credential presentation API for verifier-specified scope queries. Current SDK lets provers choose what to reveal; enterprise verifiers need to specify 'prove you have bits 0+2' and get back a yes/no with a ZK proof. This is a thin wrapper over existing requiredScopeMask but needs a clean request/response format.

OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect bridge: Agent Passport is OAuth-native. Enterprises won't rip out their OAuth infra. Build a token exchange flow where a Bolyra ZK proof is exchanged for a short-lived OAuth token scoped to the proven permissions. This is the adoption wedge.

Privacy comparison test vectors: side-by-side demonstrations showing exactly what metadata leaks in an Agent Passport flow vs a Bolyra flow for identical permission checks. These become the sales engineering artifact.

Verifier registry contract: a lightweight on-chain registry where enterprise verifiers register their accepted scope masks, so agents can pre-compute proofs for known verifier requirements. Reduces proof latency in the enterprise auth hot path.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical instance of the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. Agent Passport and AID are building the identity plumbing; Bolyra slots underneath as the privacy engine. The play is NOT 'replace Agent Passport' — it's 'Agent Passport verifiers upgrade to Bolyra proofs and stop leaking metadata.' The OAuth bridge makes this concrete: enterprises keep their Agent Passport integration, add Bolyra as the proof backend, and get selective disclosure for free. This positions Bolyra as infrastructure rather than competitor, which is critical because Agent Passport has the ecosystem momentum and Bolyra has the cryptographic moat. The IETF draft and on-chain verification give credibility signals that pure-OAuth approaches can't match in regulated verticals (fintech, healthcare) where 'prove without revealing' isn't a nice-to-have but a compliance requirement.

deliverables.name: Scope-query presentation API
deliverables.description: Thin SDK wrapper: verifier sends a ScopeQuery (required permission bits + nonce), prover returns a ZK proof of membership. Wraps existing requiredScopeMask in AgentPolicy with a clean request/response shape: createScopeResponse(credential, scopeQuery) → ScopeProof

deliverables.name: Agent Passport compatibility profile
deliverables.description: Mapping document showing how Bolyra selective-disclosure proofs satisfy Agent Passport / AID verification semantics. Defines field-by-field translation: AP's agent_id → Bolyra nullifierHash, AP's permissions → scopeMask proof, AP's operator → omitted (privacy gain). Includes a comparison table of what each protocol reveals to the verifier.

deliverables.name: Privacy comparison demo
deliverables.description: CLI script that runs the same 3 scenarios (check permission, verify delegation chain, confirm human-binding) side-by-side: shows what Agent Passport would expose vs what Bolyra reveals. Produces a markdown report. No UI — just a compelling terminal output for blog/conference use.

deliverables.name: Selective disclosure blog post
deliverables.description: Technical blog post: 'Agent Identity Without the Metadata Leak — How ZKP Selective Disclosure Beats OAuth-Style Agent Passports'. Walks through the 3 demo scenarios, explains the circuit mechanics, links to the compatibility profile. Educational framing, no CTA.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry scope-query event
deliverables.description: Add a ScopeVerified event to the on-chain registry so verifiers can check scope proofs without querying the operator's infrastructure. Minor addition to existing verifyHandshake — emit event with scopeMask hash and proof validity, no new storage.

risks: Agent Passport is early-stage and may pivot, making the compatibility profile stale before it matters
Enterprise buyers evaluating agent identity may not yet care about privacy — 'works with existing IdP' beats 'private but unfamiliar'
Selective disclosure adds proof generation latency (~200ms snarkjs, ~15ms rapidsnark) that OAuth token inspection doesn't have
Solo founder capacity: 9 days assumes no interruptions from other Bolyra/ZKProva/GeniusComply work
mvp.success_criteria: SDK scope-query API passes integration tests with all 8 permission bits, compatibility profile is published to spec/ and referenced from the IETF draft, demo script runs end-to-end producing a diff report, and at least one HN/dev-community post gets substantive technical engagement (>5 comments discussing the privacy tradeoff)

title: Cryptographic Agent Identity for Platform Load Management
description: GitHub's agent traffic crisis broke enterprise SLAs because the platform couldn't distinguish or throttle misbehaving agent clients. Bolyra's nullifier-based identity lets platforms rate-limit per-agent-identity without storing API keys, enabling shed-load decisions based on verified agent provenance.
source: GitHub AI agent crisis / platform stability issues

rationale.demand: GitHub's agent traffic issues are real and widely reported, but web search evidence returned zero URLs — no named buyers, no RFPs, no procurement cycles for cryptographic agent identity specifically, putting this squarely in anecdotal territory.
rationale.timing: GitHub's rate-limiting crisis creates a plausible near-term hook and agent auth standards are in motion, but no competitor has shipped a ZKP-based rate-limiting product, and no standard milestone directly targets this use case within 6 months.
rationale.fit: Strong primitive mapping — AgentPolicy, nullifier-as-rate-limit-key, Delegation for sub-agent chains, and IdentityRegistry all apply directly with minor modifications, though the unilateral 'load-identity' mode is new work not yet in the spec.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is achievable in ~10 days reusing existing circuits and SDK infra, but the middleware package and adoption by actual platforms adds go-to-market risk beyond pure engineering effort.

mapped_primitives.usage: Each agent proves it holds a valid credential with operator-signed permissions and unexpired timestamp. The platform verifies the PLONK proof to confirm agent provenance without learning the operator's private key or full permission set — only that the agent is authorized and within scope.

The nullifier mechanism (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) is directly applicable: scope = platform endpoint or rate-limit bucket. Same agent hitting the same endpoint produces the same nullifier, enabling per-identity rate limiting without storing API keys or correlating across endpoints.

On-chain agentTree membership proves the credential was enrolled by a legitimate operator. Root history buffer (30 entries) allows slightly stale proofs during high-traffic bursts without on-chain reads per request. Platform can cache recent roots and verify offline.

Sub-agents spawned by a primary agent (e.g., Devin spawning child workers) prove delegation chains. Platform can enforce hop-count limits (max 3) and scope narrowing — a sub-agent can't escalate beyond its parent's rate-limit tier.

SDK's createAgentCredential + proveHandshake become the client-side integration points. Agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI) attach the proof to HTTP headers. verifyHandshake is the platform-side check.

new_components_needed.description: Rate-Limit Nullifier Profile: a lightweight spec defining how platforms derive scope from (endpoint, time-window) so nullifiers partition into rate-limit buckets. Extends draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a new 'load-identity' handshake mode that returns a deterministic nullifier without requiring mutual proof.

Unilateral agent proof mode: current SDK requires mutual handshake (human + agent). Platform load management needs agent-only proof with nullifier output. Add proveAgentIdentity(agent, scope, nonce) → {proof, nullifier} that skips the human side.

HTTP middleware (Express/Fastify/nginx plugin) that extracts Bolyra proof from Authorization header, verifies it, extracts nullifier, and feeds it into a sliding-window rate limiter keyed on nullifier rather than IP or API key.

Optional on-chain reputation accumulator: platforms post nullifier-keyed penalty events (e.g., 429 violations). Other platforms can query an agent's cross-platform reputation without learning its identity. Uses nullifier unlinkability across scopes to preserve privacy.
Agent Load Identity DID method extension: did:bolyra agent identifiers gain a 'load-identity' service endpoint that platforms can resolve to discover supported rate-limit nullifier scopes.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. GitHub/Azure/AWS don't want to build ZKP infrastructure — they want a drop-in solution that lets them shed load without privacy liability. Bolyra slots in as the identity layer that platforms consume: agents prove who they are without platforms storing credentials or correlating behavior across services. The nullifier model is strictly superior to API-key-based rate limiting (no key rotation, no storage, no correlation attack surface) and to IP-based limiting (agents share IPs via cloud providers). Incumbents get better load management; Bolyra becomes the invisible trust root for every agent-to-platform interaction.

deliverables.name: Rate-Limit Nullifier Profile spec
deliverables.description: Lightweight extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining how platforms derive scope from (endpoint, time-window) tuples so nullifiers partition into rate-limit buckets. Covers the unilateral 'load-identity' handshake mode where only the agent proves — no mutual proof required.

deliverables.name: proveAgentIdentity SDK function
deliverables.description: Add proveAgentIdentity(agent, scope, nonce) → {proof, nullifier} to @bolyra/sdk. Reuses existing AgentPolicy circuit and snarkjs/rapidsnark proving pipeline. Skips human side — agent generates a scoped nullifier via Poseidon2(scope, secret) and proves credential validity in one shot. Corresponding verifyAgentIdentity on the platform side.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy circuit + existing SDK proving infra

deliverables.name: Python SDK bridge for proveAgentIdentity
deliverables.description: Expose proveAgentIdentity through the existing subprocess bridge in sdk-python so LangChain/CrewAI integrations can call it natively.

deliverables.name: Express/Fastify rate-limit middleware
deliverables.description: NPM package @bolyra/ratelimit-middleware. Extracts proof from Authorization: Bolyra <base64> header, calls verifyAgentIdentity, extracts nullifier, feeds into a sliding-window counter keyed on nullifier. Returns 429 with Retry-After when exceeded. In-memory store default, Redis adapter optional.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo: GitHub-style API with agent load shedding
deliverables.description: Minimal Express API (3 endpoints) protected by the middleware. Demo script spawns 5 agents with different credentials hitting the same endpoint — shows per-identity rate limiting working (misbehaving agent gets 429, others unaffected). Includes a delegated sub-agent to show hop-count enforcement. All local, no real GitHub integration.

deliverables.name: Technical blog post
deliverables.description: 'Solving the Agent Traffic Crisis with Cryptographic Load Identity' — explains the nullifier-as-rate-limit-key insight, walks through the demo, compares to API-key-based approaches. Published on bolyra.ai/blog. No pricing, no CTA, purely technical.

risks: Web search evidence returned nothing — demand signal is inferred from GitHub's public statements, not validated by actual enterprise buyer conversations
Unilateral agent proof mode changes the trust model (no mutual handshake) — need to ensure nullifier unlinkability still holds when scope is platform-controlled rather than jointly derived

Enterprise platforms (GitHub, Vercel) may prefer simpler solutions (just better API key management) over ZKP-based identity — the cryptographic elegance may not clear the 'is this worth integrating?' bar
Rate-limit nullifier scope derivation (endpoint × time-window) needs careful design to prevent nullifier grinding attacks where agents vary scope parameters to get fresh identities
13 days assumes no circuit modifications — if AgentPolicy needs changes to support unilateral mode cleanly, add 3-5 days for recompile + trusted setup
mvp.success_criteria: Demo video showing 5 concurrent agents hitting the same endpoint where one misbehaving agent gets rate-limited (429) while others continue unaffected — with all identity verification happening via ZK proofs and nullifiers, zero API keys stored. Secondary: at least one agent-framework maintainer (LangChain, CrewAI) engages with the spec or middleware on GitHub.

title: Privacy-Preserving Human-Agent Linking Protocol
description: BillionsNetwork's verified-agent-identity uses signed DID challenges for human-agent linking but exposes the human's DID to the agent operator. Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit proves the human endorsed the agent without revealing which human, using nullifier-based uniqueness — directly solving the linking problem the market is building toward.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and the only source is a single GitHub repo (BillionsNetwork) with no engagement metrics, named buyers, or independent corroboration.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the single repo has no shipping timeline and the timing assessment itself says 18+ months out.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) map directly with no modification needed, and the SDK already exposes proveHandshake/verifyHandshake — this is essentially a relabeling of existing capability.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is 5 days of mostly demo/docs/spec work reusing existing primitives with only minor SDK wrappers, making execution low-risk.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive for the linking problem. Human generates a nullifier scoped to the agent's credential commitment (or operator DID). This proves 'a unique human endorsed this agent' without revealing which human. The nullifier prevents the same human from endorsing the same agent twice (sybil resistance) while remaining unlinkable across different agent endorsements.

The agent side of the link. Agent proves it holds a valid, non-expired credential with the required permission bits. The selective disclosure capability means the agent can prove it has SIGN_ON_BEHALF (bit 5) without revealing its full permission set — critical for the linking handshake where the verifier only needs to confirm the agent is authorized to act.

Enables the endorsed-agent to further delegate narrowed scope to sub-agents while preserving the human endorsement chain. BillionsNetwork's DID challenge approach has no delegation story — each sub-agent would need a fresh human interaction. Bolyra's delegation circuit proves scope narrowing without revealing the original human or the full permission set.

On-chain verification of the linking proof via atomic handshake verification. The dual Merkle tree design (humanTree + agentTree) already supports the enrollment model. Nonce equality enforcement prevents replay of linking proofs. Root history buffers allow slightly stale proofs (accommodates latency between proof generation and on-chain submission).

proveHandshake() already implements the bilateral proof flow needed for linking — human proves uniqueness, agent proves policy, both bind to the same nonce. verifyHandshake() validates both sides atomically. The existing error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError) cover the failure modes of a linking protocol.

The IETF draft already specifies the mutual ZKP auth flow. Human-agent linking is a named use case within the draft's scope. The wire format (proof envelope + nonce binding) maps directly to a linking attestation format.

The DID method can represent linked human-agent pairs as DID relationships without exposing the human's identifier. A linked agent's DID document could include a verificationMethod that references the nullifier hash (proving human endorsement) without containing the human's DID — the exact privacy gap BillionsNetwork's approach fails to close.

new_components_needed.description: Linking attestation format: a standardized envelope that bundles (humanProof, agentProof, nonce, scope, timestamp) into a portable, verifiable linking attestation. Should align with the IETF draft wire format and include a revocation mechanism (human can revoke endorsement by publishing a revocation nullifier scoped to the agent).

High-level linkAgent(humanIdentity, agentCredential, scope) convenience function that wraps proveHandshake with linking-specific defaults (scope set to agent's credentialCommitment, permissions defaulting to SIGN_ON_BEHALF). Returns a LinkingAttestation object with revoke() method.

BillionsNetwork compatibility adapter: accepts their DID challenge format as input, translates to a Bolyra linking flow, and returns a response that their verified-agent-identity consumers can verify. This is the trojan horse — drop-in replacement that upgrades their privacy model.

Revocation sub-protocol: human publishes a revocation nullifier (Poseidon2(revocation_scope, secret)) to unlink from an agent. Needs a sparse nullifier set on-chain or a revocation accumulator. Without this, linking is irrevocable, which is a non-starter for production use.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. BillionsNetwork is building the market category (verified agent identity) and acquiring early adopters. Their architecture has a known, structural privacy flaw (human DID exposure) that they cannot fix without rebuilding around ZKPs. Bolyra slots in as the privacy layer: (1) a compatibility adapter lets their existing users upgrade without switching stacks, (2) the IETF draft gives Bolyra standards legitimacy that a startup repo cannot match, (3) once the linking attestation format is adopted, the delegation circuit becomes the natural extension — locking in Bolyra as the trust infrastructure beneath their application layer. The revocation sub-protocol is the long-term moat: any competitor that copies the linking flow still needs to solve revocation, which requires either Bolyra's nullifier algebra or a bespoke ZKP accumulator.

deliverables.name: linking-example
deliverables.description: End-to-end demo script showing human-agent linking via existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake — human proves uniqueness scoped to agent's operator DID, agent proves policy, verifier confirms link without learning human identity. Wraps existing SDK calls with clear narrative comments.

deliverables.name: human-agent-linking spec section
deliverables.description: Add a 'Human-Agent Linking' section to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md formalizing the nullifier-scoped-to-operator pattern as a named protocol flow. Define the linking attestation format (proof envelope + nullifier hash + agent credential commitment). Reference BillionsNetwork's DID-challenge approach as the non-private baseline.

deliverables.name: linking test vectors
deliverables.description: Add test vectors to spec/test-vectors/ covering: (1) valid link, (2) replay detection via nullifier collision, (3) expired agent credential rejection, (4) scope escalation rejection. Machine-readable JSON consumed by conformance runner.

deliverables.name: blog post: linking without leaking
deliverables.description: Technical blog post comparing DID-challenge linking (BillionsNetwork pattern) vs nullifier-based linking (Bolyra). Show the privacy gap with a concrete example. Pure educational content — no CTA, no pricing.

deliverables.name: SDK linking convenience wrapper
deliverables.description: Add linkAgentToHuman() and verifyLink() thin wrappers in @bolyra/sdk that call proveHandshake/verifyHandshake with the correct nullifier scope (operator DID as external nullifier). Typed return includes nullifierHash for on-chain anchoring. No new circuit logic.

risks: BillionsNetwork's approach may evolve to add privacy features, reducing the differentiation window
Demand signal is weak (score 15) — the market may not yet care about privacy in human-agent linking
Grey zone risk on the blog post if it reads as marketing rather than technical education — keep it purely comparative with code examples
Nullifier-scoped-to-operator-DID is a novel pattern that hasn't been validated by external cryptographers yet
mvp.success_criteria: Demo script runs end-to-end in under 5 seconds (fast mode), spec section passes editorial review, test vectors pass conformance runner, and at least one external developer (GitHub issue or Discord) references the linking flow within 60 days of publication.

title: ZKP Upgrade for HTTP Message Signature Gateways
description: HUMAN Security's Verified AI Agent framework uses RFC 9421 HTTP signatures at the gateway, but every request reveals the signing key's identity. Bolyra's gateway can verify agent ZKP proofs instead, proving the agent is authorized without linking requests to a persistent public key — defeating cross-service tracking.
source: HUMAN Verified AI Agent open-source framework

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs were provided; HUMAN's Verified AI Agent framework exists but no named buyers or procurement signals for ZKP-based gateway auth were cited.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were concretely cited; RFC 9421 is published but the ZKP-upgrade demand timeline is speculative with no anchored events.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation) plus IdentityRegistry and SDK map directly with only minor modifications needed, representing genuine ZKP advantage over persistent-key HTTP signatures.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec is well-scoped at ~12 days reusing existing PLONK verifiers and SDK internals, but the truncated spec and failed web search leave integration complexity and adoption path partially unvalidated.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive. The agent proves it holds a valid operator-signed credential with the required permission bits (e.g. READ_DATA, WRITE_DATA) without revealing the operator's public key or the full bitmask. The gateway verifies the PLONK proof instead of an RFC 9421 HTTP signature, so no persistent public key is exposed across requests.

Optional upgrade path. When a gateway needs to verify that a human delegated to the agent (not just that the agent has a valid credential), the human's Groth16 proof anchors the trust chain. The nullifier's scope-binding means the human is unlinkable across different gateway deployments.

Enables the full value prop: human → agent delegation with scope narrowing. A gateway can require proof that the agent's permissions were narrowed from a human principal, not self-issued. The one-way scope narrowing enforced in-circuit prevents an agent from escalating beyond what the human authorized.

The gateway can optionally check on-chain root history to confirm the agent's credential commitment is in the current agent Merkle tree, providing revocation support. The 30-entry root history buffer gives a ~30-block grace period for in-flight proofs during tree updates.

Gateway integration calls verifyHandshake() or a new single-proof verify function. The existing proveHandshake flow bundles human+agent proofs with nonce binding, which maps directly to per-request gateway verification with replay protection.

new_components_needed.description: HTTP gateway middleware (Express/Fastify/nginx-lua) that intercepts inbound requests, extracts the Bolyra proof from a new HTTP header (e.g. Bolyra-Proof), verifies it via the SDK, and forwards on success. Replaces RFC 9421 Signature/Signature-Input headers. Must handle proof deserialization, nonce freshness checks, and permission-scope matching against route-level policies.

Single-proof verification mode: verifyAgentProof(proof, requiredScope, nonce) that skips the human side for pure agent-to-service calls. Currently the SDK only exposes the paired verifyHandshake. This is a thin wrapper over the existing PLONK verifier.

HTTP binding specification: defines which headers carry the proof, how the nonce is derived (e.g. from request method + path + timestamp), and how a gateway advertises required permission scopes via a WWW-Authenticate challenge. Should reference draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 and position as a privacy-preserving alternative to RFC 9421 Section 3.

HUMAN Verified AI Agent compatibility shim: adapter that lets existing HUMAN framework deployments add Bolyra as an alternative verification backend alongside their existing RFC 9421 path, enabling incremental migration.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. HUMAN Security has enterprise distribution and brand trust for bot detection — they define the gateway pattern and the HTTP signature format. Bolyra doesn't compete with their gateway; it replaces the verification primitive inside it. HUMAN's framework is open-source, so Bolyra can ship a drop-in verification backend that upgrades their architecture from 'signed and linkable' to 'proven and unlinkable' without requiring HUMAN to change their gateway routing, policy engine, or customer contracts. This positions Bolyra as infrastructure that makes incumbents' products better rather than a competitor, which is the fastest path to enterprise adoption. The cross-service unlinkability story is also a regulatory tailwind: as EU AI Act and CCPA enforcement tighten, enterprises will need to prove their AI agents aren't being tracked across services — Bolyra's nullifier scoping provides this by construction.

deliverables.name: verifyAgentProof SDK function
deliverables.description: Single-proof verification mode: verifyAgentProof(proof, requiredPermissions, nonce) that verifies an AgentPolicy PLONK proof without requiring the paired human proof. Thin wrapper over existing PLONK verifier in @bolyra/sdk.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy PLONK verifier + existing sdk verify internals

deliverables.name: HTTP proof binding spec
deliverables.description: Specification defining Bolyra-Proof and Bolyra-Nonce HTTP headers, proof serialization format (base64url-encoded JSON), nonce derivation (SHA-256 of method + path + timestamp window), required permission scope declaration via Bolyra-Scope header, and error response codes. Positioned as companion to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.

deliverables.name: Express gateway middleware
deliverables.description: @bolyra/gateway-middleware Express/Connect middleware. Extracts Bolyra-Proof header, deserializes proof, checks nonce freshness (configurable window, default 30s), matches route-level permission policies, calls verifyAgentProof, forwards on success or returns 401/403. Drops in where RFC 9421 signature verification currently sits.

deliverables.name: Gateway demo
deliverables.description: Standalone Express server with 3 protected routes at different permission tiers (READ_DATA, WRITE_DATA, FINANCIAL_SMALL). Includes a client script that generates AgentPolicy proofs and makes authenticated requests. README shows side-by-side comparison: RFC 9421 (key exposed) vs Bolyra (ZKP, unlinkable).
deliverables.reuses: existing examples/mcp-demo patterns + AgentPolicy test fixtures

deliverables.name: Integration guide
deliverables.description: How-to guide: migrating an HTTP signature gateway to Bolyra ZKP verification. Covers middleware installation, route policy configuration, nonce window tuning, and optional upgrade path to full human-delegated proofs via Delegation circuit.

risks: Proof size overhead: a Groth16/PLONK proof (~800B-1.2KB base64) in an HTTP header per request adds latency vs a 256B Ed25519 signature — must benchmark and document the tradeoff
Nonce freshness window is a UX/security tradeoff: too tight and clock skew causes false rejections, too loose and replay window grows — needs tuning guidance in docs
No web search evidence of actual demand — HUMAN Security's Verified AI Agent framework is new and adoption is unproven, so this MVP could land with zero users
Gateway middleware is Express-only initially — real enterprise gateways run nginx/Envoy/Kong, so the Express version is a proof-of-concept, not a production integration
EAD grey zone means no outbound promotion — the middleware ships but sits undiscovered until EAD clears or organic discovery happens
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can npm install @bolyra/gateway-middleware, add 3 lines to an Express app, define per-route permission policies, and have agent requests verified via ZKP proofs instead of HTTP signatures — demonstrated end-to-end in the demo with proof generation, transmission, and verification completing in under 500ms on a laptop.

title: ZKP Security Training Module for Agentic AI
description: GitHub's Secure Code Game teaches developers to find auth flaws in agentic AI. A Bolyra-sponsored challenge level demonstrating ZKP vs token-based agent auth would drive developer awareness and position Bolyra as the security-first choice, reaching GitHub's massive developer audience.
source: GitHub Secure Code Game for agentic AI security

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs, named buyers, or engagement metrics were provided — demand is entirely speculative.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or concrete timeline anchors cited; GitHub Secure Code Game's agentic AI focus is assumed but undated.
rationale.fit: All four core primitives (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation, IdentityRegistry) map directly to challenge vulnerability categories with no circuit modifications needed, and the SDK error classes align to exploit categories.
rationale.feasibility: 8-day MVP with 75% reuse of existing primitives and a well-scoped deliverable set is realistic, though the mock proving mode is new work and GitHub partnership acceptance is uncertain.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core demonstration primitive — the challenge level would show how AgentPolicy's selective disclosure and cumulative-bit permissions prevent the exact over-permissioned-token attacks that Secure Code Game teaches devs to find. Players discover that a leaked bearer token grants full access, then see how AgentPolicy proves only the required permission bits without revealing the full bitmask.

Second challenge stage — demonstrate sybil resistance. Players exploit a token-based system where one user creates multiple agent identities, then see how HumanUniqueness nullifiers (scoped, unlinkable) prevent this without revealing the human's identity.

Advanced challenge level — players exploit an unbounded delegation chain (agent delegates to sub-agent with escalated permissions). Bolyra's scope-narrowing and 3-hop-max enforcement is the fix. Shows bitwise subset enforcement and expiry narrowing as defense.

On-chain verification reference — the challenge can show how atomic handshake verification and nonce equality enforcement prevent replay attacks that players exploit in the vulnerable version. Nonce binding is the key teaching moment.

The SDK's error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, StaleProofError, InvalidPermissionError) map directly to vulnerability categories players would discover. The challenge's 'fix' code would use createAgentCredential + verifyHandshake as the secure alternative.

new_components_needed.description: secure-code-game challenge package — a self-contained level with vulnerable Flask/Express app (token-based agent auth), exploit scripts, and Bolyra-secured version using @bolyra/sdk. Needs test harness compatible with GitHub's Secure Code Game format (hack.py / solution files).

Simplified 'playground' mode for the SDK that runs without circuit artifacts — mock proofs that demonstrate the API surface and permission model without requiring ptau/zkey downloads. Needed so challenge participants can run locally without 200MB of artifacts.

Educational writeup mapping each vulnerability to OWASP Agentic top-10 categories (already partially done in docs/owasp-agentic-mapping.md) with Bolyra-specific mitigations. This becomes the challenge's learning material.

positioning_alignment: Strong alignment with privacy-layer-under-incumbents. GitHub's Secure Code Game reaches millions of developers who build ON TOP of platforms (LangChain, CrewAI, OpenAI). By sponsoring a challenge level, Bolyra positions as the security primitive these developers should demand from their frameworks — not as a competing platform but as the missing auth layer. This is the exact 'Intel Inside' positioning: developers learn that ZKP-based agent auth exists, then pressure framework vendors to integrate it. The educational framing also sidesteps the cold-start problem — developers learn the API in a game context before needing it in production, building familiarity without requiring immediate adoption.

deliverables.name: secure-code-challenge
deliverables.description: Self-contained challenge level with vulnerable Express app (bearer-token agent auth with over-permissioned tokens, replay attacks, unbounded delegation) plus exploit scripts and Bolyra-secured solution using @bolyra/sdk. Follows GitHub Secure Code Game format (Level/hack.py + solution pattern). Three stages: token theft → sybil attack → delegation escalation.

deliverables.name: sdk-playground-mode
deliverables.description: Mock proving mode for @bolyra/sdk — flag that skips ptau/zkey downloads and uses deterministic mock proofs. Preserves full API surface and permission validation (cumulative-bit checks, scope narrowing, expiry) but removes the 200MB artifact requirement. Lets challenge participants npm-install and run immediately.

deliverables.name: owasp-agentic-walkthrough
deliverables.description: Educational writeup mapping each challenge vulnerability to OWASP Agentic top-10 categories, extending existing docs/owasp-agentic-mapping.md. Each vulnerability gets: threat description, exploit code reference, Bolyra mitigation, and which circuit enforces it. Pure technical education, no CTAs.

deliverables.name: challenge-test-harness
deliverables.description: CI-compatible test harness that validates both the vulnerable app (exploits succeed) and the secured app (exploits fail). Runs in GitHub Actions so maintainers can verify the challenge works. Also serves as the acceptance test for the challenge itself.

risks: GitHub Secure Code Game may not accept external challenge contributions — no public contribution process exists yet, so this could end up as a standalone repo exercise rather than an official level
Developer attention is competed for heavily; a ZKP challenge may feel too niche compared to SQL injection or XSS levels that affect more developers daily
Mock proving mode could give a false sense of simplicity — developers who try real proofs later hit the 200MB artifact wall and churn
8 days is optimistic if the Secure Code Game format requires custom infrastructure beyond a simple Express app + test harness
mvp.success_criteria: Challenge repo gets 50+ stars or forks within 60 days of publication; at least 3 developers complete the full three-stage challenge (measured by solution PR submissions or GitHub Discussions engagement); playground mode is merged into @bolyra/sdk mainline and used by at least one other example or integration beyond this challenge.

title: ZKP Pre-Agent-Steps for CI/CD Agent Authentication
description: GitHub's pre-agent-steps hook runs custom auth setup before agents execute. Bolyra could provide a pre-agent-step action that generates a scoped ZKP proof of the agent's permissions, replacing static tokens with per-session proofs that auto-expire and can't be replayed.
source: GitHub Agentic Workflows weekly update — pre-agent-steps

rationale.demand: Web search returned zero evidence; GitHub pre-agent-steps is a nascent feature with no verifiable public demand for ZKP-based CI agent auth — the preliminary score of 16 had no URLs backing it.
rationale.timing: No competitor launches, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines could be verified; pre-agent-steps itself is early-stage with no confirmed GA timeline, making the 'closing window' framing speculative.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and the SDK map cleanly — scoped permissions, expiry, and nonce binding are native Bolyra primitives requiring only minor integration work, though the use case substitutes ZKP where a simpler OIDC/JWT approach likely suffices for most CI teams.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP is well-scoped at ~8 days using existing circuits and SDK with PLONK (no ceremony), but depends on GitHub shipping and stabilizing pre-agent-steps hooks, which is outside Bolyra's control.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core proving primitive. The GitHub Action generates an AgentPolicy PLONK proof at the start of pre-agent-steps, binding the CI agent's model hash, operator key (GitHub org), and scoped permissions (e.g. READ_DATA + WRITE_DATA for a PR bot, FINANCIAL_SMALL for a deploy bot). The expiry timestamp is set to the workflow run timeout, giving auto-expiration for free.

Enables the org owner (human) → CI agent delegation chain. The org admin holds the root credential; the GitHub Action proves a delegation token that narrows scope to exactly the permissions the workflow needs. Max 3 hops covers org-owner → repo-admin → CI-agent naturally.
Used once during org setup: the human admin proves uniqueness to anchor the delegation chain root. Not invoked per-workflow-run — only when bootstrapping the org's Bolyra identity.

On-chain verification endpoint. The pre-agent-step proof can be verified against the registry's agentTree root and delegation nullifier replay protection. The 30-entry root history buffer accommodates the lag between proof generation in CI and downstream verification.
The GitHub Action wraps createAgentCredential() and proveHandshake() calls. The action outputs the serialized proof as a step output that downstream steps/services consume via verifyHandshake().

new_components_needed.description: GitHub Action (bolyra/pre-agent-step) that wraps the SDK: reads org credentials from GitHub secrets, generates a scoped AgentPolicy proof, and exports it as BOLYRA_PROOF step output. Needs to bundle snarkjs WASM prover (not rapidsnark) since GitHub runners are ephemeral Linux VMs.

Lightweight proof serialization format for CI artifacts — a compact base64url encoding of (proof, publicSignals, nonce) that can be passed as an environment variable or step output without hitting shell escaping issues.

GitHub-specific profile of draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining the CI agent identity model: how GitHub org maps to operator key, how workflow permissions map to the 8-bit bitmask, and how run timeout maps to expiry.

Verification middleware for downstream services (API gateways, deploy targets) that validates the BOLYRA_PROOF from the CI agent before granting access — analogous to the existing OpenClaw adapter but for generic HTTP services.

positioning_alignment: Directly reinforces the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. GitHub is the incumbent CI/CD platform; Bolyra slots in as an invisible auth layer via a marketplace Action. GitHub agents currently use static PATs or GITHUB_TOKEN with coarse org-level scopes — Bolyra replaces these with per-session, scope-narrowed, auto-expiring proofs that GitHub never needs to change its architecture to support. The delegation chain model (human org-owner → CI agent) is a natural enterprise upsell path: security teams get cryptographic proof that no CI agent exceeded its granted permissions, with on-chain audit trail. This is the same wedge pattern as the OpenClaw integration — ride the incumbent's distribution, own the trust layer.

deliverables.name: proof-envelope-base64url
deliverables.description: Compact base64url serialization of (proof, publicSignals, nonce) tuple that survives shell variable expansion and GitHub step output piping. Add serialize/deserialize to @bolyra/sdk.

deliverables.name: bolyra/pre-agent-step GitHub Action
deliverables.description: Composite GitHub Action that reads BOLYRA_OPERATOR_KEY and BOLYRA_PERMISSIONS from secrets/inputs, calls createAgentCredential() + proveHandshake() via bundled snarkjs WASM prover, and exports BOLYRA_PROOF as a step output. Uses AgentPolicy PLONK (no ceremony needed). Expiry defaults to github.event.repository.default_branch == 'main' ? 1h : 15m.

deliverables.name: verify-proof companion action
deliverables.description: Lightweight GitHub Action (or standalone Node script) that downstream steps call to verify the BOLYRA_PROOF output. Checks proof validity, expiry, permission bitmask, and nonce freshness. No on-chain call — pure snarkjs verify against bundled vkey.

deliverables.name: ci-agent-profile spec
deliverables.description: One-page GitHub-specific profile of draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01: maps GitHub org ID to operator key derivation, workflow permission labels to 8-bit bitmask values, and run timeout to proof expiry. Covers the 3 common archetypes: PR-bot (READ+WRITE), deploy-bot (READ+WRITE+FINANCIAL_SMALL), admin-bot (full delegation).

deliverables.name: end-to-end demo workflow
deliverables.description: Public repo with a .github/workflows/demo.yml that uses bolyra/pre-agent-step, then a downstream job that verifies the proof before deploying a dummy service. README walks through the output logs showing proof generation, serialization, and verification.

deliverables.name: docs: quickstart guide
deliverables.description: docs/integrations/github-pre-agent-step.md — setup instructions covering: (1) generate operator keypair, (2) store in GitHub secrets, (3) add the action to pre-agent-steps, (4) consume BOLYRA_PROOF in downstream steps. Includes permission mapping table.

risks: snarkjs WASM proving in GitHub runners may be slow (15-30s for PLONK) — could annoy users with long pre-agent-step times; mitigate by defaulting to AgentPolicy PLONK which is smaller than Delegation
GitHub pre-agent-steps is new and the API surface may change — pin to a specific GitHub Actions runner version and document the minimum required version

Without on-chain verification the proof is only as trustworthy as the vkey bundled in the action — a compromised action could ship a fake vkey; mitigate with GitHub Action pinning (SHA-based) and publishing vkey checksums
Demand signal is unvalidated (web search failed) — this may be a solution looking for a problem; the demo repo's GitHub stars and forks in the first 30 days will be the real signal
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can add bolyra/pre-agent-step@v1 to their workflow, configure two secrets, and have every CI agent run produce a verifiable, scoped, auto-expiring ZKP proof in under 30 seconds — demonstrated end-to-end in the public demo repo with passing CI.

title: Bolyra SDK as LangChain Agent Auth Provider
description: LangChain's Agent Auth beta supports OAuth 2.0 flows but lacks zero-knowledge delegation. A Bolyra adapter for LangChain's authenticate() API would let any LangChain agent prove scoped permissions via ZKP, reaching the largest agentic framework ecosystem with minimal integration friction.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were cited — the claim of LangChain Agent Auth beta with an authenticate() API is unverified, and no named buyers, GitHub issues, or engagement metrics support demand above anecdotal.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or concrete timeline anchors were provided; LangChain's agent auth is described as 'beta' without a version number, publication date, or GA timeline, making the window purely speculative.
rationale.fit: All four Bolyra primitives (AgentPolicy, Delegation, HumanUniqueness, IdentityRegistry) map directly with no circuit modifications needed, and the SDK pipeline is a clean backend for the adapter — but the score is capped because the LangChain authenticate() API itself is unverified, so the mapping is against an assumed interface.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP spec is well-scoped at ~8 days reusing existing SDK and circuits with no new cryptographic work, but feasibility depends on LangChain actually shipping a stable auth provider interface, which is unconfirmed.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core proving primitive — LangChain agents use AgentPolicy to prove scoped permissions (e.g. READ_DATA, FINANCIAL_SMALL) via PLONK proof instead of OAuth tokens. The selective disclosure capability maps directly to LangChain's authenticate() scopes: agent proves it has the required permission bits without revealing the full bitmask.

Enables LangChain's multi-agent patterns (AgentExecutor → sub-agents, LangGraph nodes) to propagate scoped credentials down the call chain. A parent agent delegates narrowed permissions to child agents, enforced on-circuit. This is the killer differentiator vs OAuth — delegation chains are cryptographically auditable without a token endpoint.

Human-in-the-loop LangChain flows (e.g. human approval steps in LangGraph) can bind the human's identity to the agent chain via the handshake protocol. Proves the human who authorized the agent is a unique member of an allowed set, without revealing identity.

On-chain verification endpoint for LangChain tool servers that want to verify agent credentials without trusting the agent's self-attestation. Tool servers call verifyHandshake on-chain or verify proofs locally using the vkey. Root history buffers (30 entries) provide a practical window for async agent workflows.

The SDK's createAgentCredential → proveHandshake → verifyHandshake pipeline is the direct backend for the LangChain adapter. The adapter is a thin wrapper that translates LangChain's authenticate(scopes) → proveHandshake(human, agent, {requiredScope}) and maps the HandshakeResult back to LangChain's AuthResult type.

Existing stub in integrations/ provides the starting skeleton. Needs to be evolved from a BaseTool wrapper (tool that agents can call) into an AuthProvider (middleware that authenticates agents themselves). The stub's existence confirms this was already on the roadmap.

new_components_needed.description: LangChain AuthProvider adapter — implements LangChain's authenticate() interface, maps OAuth-style scopes to Bolyra permission bitmasks, manages credential lifecycle (create on init, prove per-request, handle expiry). Must support both LangChain and LangGraph (the multi-agent orchestrator). This is the primary deliverable.

Scope-to-bitmask mapping utility — a declarative config that maps LangChain-style string scopes ('read:data', 'financial:small') to Bolyra's 8-bit cumulative permission encoding. Thin but important for DX; without it developers must manually compute bitmasks.

LangGraph delegation middleware — for multi-agent LangGraph workflows, automatically narrows delegation at each graph edge. When a LangGraph node spawns a sub-agent, the middleware creates a delegated credential with scope ≤ parent. Uses the existing Delegation circuit.

Bolyra-LangChain binding spec — documents the wire format for how Bolyra proofs travel through LangChain's callback/metadata system, how tool servers verify them, and the trust model (what's verified on-chain vs locally). Needed for ecosystem credibility and third-party tool server adoption.

Verifier middleware for LangChain tool servers — a FastAPI/Express middleware that tool servers install to verify incoming Bolyra proofs from LangChain agents. Checks proof validity, permission scope, expiry, and delegation chain depth. Complements the agent-side AuthProvider.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. LangChain owns the developer surface (800K+ monthly downloads, dominant agentic framework); Bolyra slots underneath as the auth primitive that LangChain doesn't want to build itself. The strategy mirrors how Stripe sat under e-commerce platforms: LangChain keeps the developer relationship, Bolyra provides the cryptographic trust layer they can't replicate. Key alignment points: (1) LangChain's Agent Auth beta explicitly acknowledges OAuth is insufficient for agent-to-agent delegation — Bolyra's Delegation circuit solves exactly this gap; (2) LangChain is framework-agnostic on auth providers, creating a clean integration surface without platform lock-in; (3) reaching LangChain's ecosystem (LangSmith, LangGraph, LangServe) through a single adapter multiplies distribution without direct GTM spend; (4) the ZKP angle gives LangChain a differentiation story against competitors (CrewAI, AutoGen) who only offer OAuth — making Bolyra's adoption a competitive advantage for LangChain itself.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/langchain-auth
deliverables.description: LangChain AuthProvider adapter implementing authenticate() interface — maps LangChain scopes to Bolyra permission bitmasks, wraps createAgentCredential/proveHandshake/verifyHandshake. Supports both LangChain AgentExecutor and LangGraph multi-agent flows. Ships as npm package.

deliverables.name: langchain-auth-spec
deliverables.description: Technical specification defining scope-to-bitmask mapping, credential lifecycle (init/prove/expire/refresh), LangGraph delegation chain semantics, and error handling. Covers AuthProvider interface contract and wire format for proof transport in LangChain's auth headers.

deliverables.name: langchain-auth-demo
deliverables.description: Working example: LangGraph multi-agent pipeline where parent agent delegates narrowed permissions to child agents via Bolyra ZKP. Shows a 3-node graph (orchestrator → researcher → writer) with permission narrowing at each hop. README with gif/screenshot.

deliverables.name: langchain-auth-docs
deliverables.description: Integration quickstart: install, configure AuthProvider, map scopes, verify proofs on tool-server side. Includes comparison table vs OAuth2 showing what ZKP delegation buys you (no token endpoint, cryptographic audit trail, one-way narrowing).

deliverables.name: langchain-auth-tests
deliverables.description: Integration test suite: mock LangChain authenticate() calls, verify proof generation/verification round-trip, test delegation chain depth 3, test expiry rejection, test scope narrowing enforcement. Uses fast witness-only mode for CI speed.

risks: LangChain Agent Auth beta API is unstable — authenticate() interface may change before GA, requiring adapter rewrites
Web search failed so demand signal is unverified — the 'Agent Auth beta' may be vapor or have pivoted since the signal was captured
Solo-founder capacity: 9.5 days is optimistic if LangChain's auth internals are poorly documented or require monkey-patching
PLONK proof size (~1KB) in HTTP auth headers may hit size limits on some LangChain tool server implementations
Dependency on LangChain's release cadence — if they ship breaking changes to auth before we stabilize, maintenance burden spikes
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can `npm install @bolyra/langchain-auth`, configure it as their LangChain AuthProvider in 3 lines, and have their LangGraph agents proving scoped ZKP credentials on every tool call — verified by: (1) integration tests pass in CI, (2) demo runs end-to-end with real proofs in <5s, (3) at least 1 external developer successfully integrates it (tracked via GitHub issue or Discord thread within 30 days of publish)

title: ZKP Layer for Microsoft Agent Mesh / IATP
description: Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit uses DIDs with Ed25519 for inter-agent trust but lacks privacy-preserving proof of permissions. Bolyra's Delegation circuit could plug in as the ZKP layer that proves scoped authorization without revealing the full credential chain, complementing IATP's transport.
source: Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit with DID-based Agent Mesh

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no verifiable URLs were provided — the Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit exists but there is no evidence of demand for a ZKP privacy layer on top of IATP from named buyers or funded competitors.
rationale.timing: IATP and Agent Mesh are early-stage Microsoft research projects without published GA timelines or standard finalization dates, placing any integration window at 18+ months.
rationale.fit: Delegation, AgentPolicy, and IdentityRegistry map well to the described use case with minor modifications, and the PLONK verifier is already deployed, though the SDK delegate() stub requires major work to become production-ready.
rationale.feasibility: The 16-day MVP estimate is plausible but depends on promoting delegate() from stub to production (6 days alone), writing a new binding spec, and building an adapter against an unstable external API with no published integration surface.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core value prop — proves scoped authorization (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) without revealing the full credential chain. IATP handles transport and DID resolution; Bolyra's Delegation circuit sits underneath to prove 'this agent has FINANCIAL_SMALL + READ_DATA permissions delegated from operator X' without exposing the operator's full bitmask or intermediate delegation hops.

Each agent in the mesh needs a verifiable credential commitment. AgentPolicy already binds an EdDSA operator signature to a permission bitmask with selective disclosure — the agent proves it holds requiredScopeMask bits without revealing the full bitmask, which is exactly what IATP's governance layer lacks.

On-chain delegation nullifier replay protection and hop-count enforcement (max 3) map directly to Agent Mesh's need for governance guardrails. The root history buffer allows asynchronous verification across mesh participants without requiring synchronous state reads.
Already deployed on Base Sepolia. IATP agents operating across the mesh can verify delegation proofs on-chain or use the exported verifier logic off-chain via snarkjs/rapidsnark.

SDK's delegate() stub (v0.3) would become the primary integration surface — Microsoft SDK consumers would call delegate() to produce a PLONK proof that an IATP transport message can carry as an attached credential.

new_components_needed.description: IATP-Bolyra binding spec: defines how a Bolyra delegation proof is encoded as a DIDComm attachment or IATP header, mapping did:bolyra identifiers to the Ed25519 DIDs that Agent Mesh already uses. Essentially a profile of draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 scoped to IATP transport.

@bolyra/agent-mesh-adapter: TypeScript adapter that wraps the Bolyra SDK for Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit. Accepts an IATP DidDocument, extracts the Ed25519 verification key, maps it to a Bolyra operator key, and produces/verifies delegation proofs. Would plug into the toolkit's trust-verification hook.

Promote delegate() from stub to full implementation in @bolyra/sdk v0.3. The delegation flow (prove → serialize → attach to IATP message → verify on receiver) must be production-ready, not just circuit-level.

Optional: DID-to-BabyJubjub key binding circuit. Microsoft uses Ed25519 keys; Bolyra circuits use BabyJubjub EdDSA. Either add an in-circuit Ed25519-to-BJJ derivation proof (expensive, ~100K+ constraints) or define a registration step where the Ed25519 DID owner signs a BJJ public key into the agent tree. The registration approach needs no new circuit, just an SDK-level ceremony.

Conformance test vectors for IATP interop: extend the existing 37-vector suite with cases covering delegation proof round-trips through DIDComm envelopes, expired delegation rejection, scope escalation rejection at the IATP layer.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Microsoft owns the transport (IATP), the identity layer (Entra + DIDs), and the governance framework — Bolyra doesn't compete with any of that. Instead, Bolyra slots in as the cryptographic proof engine that makes IATP's authorization claims privacy-preserving and verifiable without a centralized policy server. Microsoft gets to say 'our agent mesh supports ZKP-verified delegated permissions' without building the ZK stack themselves. Bolyra gets distribution through the Azure/Microsoft agent ecosystem without needing direct enterprise sales. The strategy mirrors how zk-SNARKs became infrastructure under Ethereum L2s — the rollup owns the user relationship, the proof system owns the trust guarantee. Key risk: Microsoft could build their own ZKP layer or acquire one, but the Semaphore v4 ceremony reuse and the patent on cumulative-bit delegation narrowing create meaningful switching costs once integrated.

deliverables.name: sdk delegate() full implementation
deliverables.description: Promote delegate() from v0.3 stub to production: prove delegation (PLONK), serialize proof to portable JSON envelope, verify on receiver side. Includes scope-narrowing validation and nullifier generation.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation circuit + DelegationPlonkVerifier + existing SDK proving pipeline

deliverables.name: IATP-Bolyra binding spec
deliverables.description: Profile of draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 scoped to IATP transport. Defines how a serialized Bolyra delegation proof is carried as a DIDComm attachment or IATP header, and how did:bolyra maps to Ed25519 DIDs used by Agent Mesh.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/agent-mesh-adapter
deliverables.description: Thin TypeScript adapter: accepts an IATP DidDocument, extracts Ed25519 verification key, maps to Bolyra operator key, calls delegate()/verifyDelegation(). Exposes a single verifyIATPAttachment() entry point.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo script
deliverables.description: CLI script: two agents in a simulated mesh exchange IATP messages with Bolyra delegation proofs attached. Agent A delegates FINANCIAL_SMALL to Agent B, Agent B proves scoped authorization to Agent C. Verifiable locally with no infra.

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Mocha tests covering: delegation proof round-trip through IATP envelope, scope narrowing enforcement, nullifier replay rejection, Ed25519-to-Bolyra key mapping edge cases.

risks: Microsoft Agent Mesh / IATP is pre-GA with unstable APIs — the adapter may need rework if their DID trust-verification hook interface changes
Web search returned zero evidence of external demand — this may be a solution looking for a problem with an 18+ month adoption horizon
delegate() promotion from stub to production is the critical path and touches circuit integration, serialization format, and SDK public API — scope creep risk is high
Solo founder spending 16 days on a speculative integration delays higher-scoring opportunities in the pipeline
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can npm install @bolyra/agent-mesh-adapter, pass it an IATP DidDocument and a permission scope, and get back a verifiable PLONK delegation proof that round-trips through a DIDComm attachment — demonstrated by the CLI demo completing without errors and the test suite passing with both mock and full proofs.
